HomeGlobal ShocksSingaporeRegional

Singapore

Domestic price transmission and sectoral activity in the Singapore economy

Energy supply

Overview

Singapore's energy complex is under severe stress: the Jurong Island refining and petrochemicals cluster has contracted sharply since the pre-conflict period, while pump prices and oil-component price indices have surged to war-period highs, confirming broad-based pass-through into domestic costs. The supply-side disruption is compounded by a structural shift in crude feedstock sourcing — Middle Eastern countries now supply over 82% of crude imports — and a near-complete cessation of Qatari LNG, which has been replaced by Australian supply, raising questions about contract continuity. Shipping flows through the Malacca Strait and at Singapore port remain materially below what would be expected in a normal year, with tanker export tonnage at its war-period low.

Key Drivers (5)

  • Petroleum refining output (IIP) is down 21.9% from the November–December 2025 average, and petrochemicals output is down 27.8%, with other chemicals off 31.2% — the Jurong Island complex is running well below pre-conflict capacity across all three sub-sectors. Data are as of March 2026 and stale by 65 days.

    ⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.petroleum_refining⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.petrochemicals⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.specialty_and_other_chemicals

  • Daily pump prices are at or near their war-period highs across all grades: RON 92 at SGD 3.42/L (97.6% through the war range), diesel at SGD 4.67/L (at the war-period high, up 77% from the pre-conflict average). The oil component of the domestic supply price index is up 64% and the oil import price index up 57% from the pre-conflict average (March 2026 data, stale 65 days).

    ⌗ sg.prices.pump_prices_daily⌗ sg.prices.sg_supply_prices⌗ sg.prices.sg_import_prices

  • Qatar's share of Singapore's LNG imports has fallen from ~43.5% to zero (March 2026), fully replaced by Australia (now 84% of LNG imports). Qatar's share of total natural gas imports has also dropped from ~18.9% to zero. This is the single largest supply-source shift visible in the trade data and removes a key Middle Eastern gas supplier entirely.

    ⌗ sg.trade.trade_pshare_sitc3431000⌗ sg.trade.trade_pshare_sitc343

  • Malacca Strait weekly transits are running 11.7% below normal for the period (4-week average gap: -14.2%), with the war-period maximum gap reaching -17.5%. Singapore tanker export tonnage is 23.6% below normal for the period and at its war-period low; tanker import tonnage is 5.5% below normal for the period. Container import and export tonnage gaps have widened to -20% and -21.4% respectively as of the week of 6 April 2026 (data stale 29 days).

    ⌗ sg.shipping.malacca_strait_weekly_transits⌗ sg.shipping.tankers⌗ sg.shipping.containers

  • Headline CPI rose to 1.8% year-on-year in March 2026 (+0.6 percentage points above the pre-conflict average), with MAS Core inflation at 1.7% (+0.5 pp). The oil component of producer prices is up 61% from the pre-conflict average, indicating upstream cost pressure has not yet fully passed through to consumer prices. All CPI and price index data are stale by 65 days.

    ⌗ sg.prices.headline_and_core_cpi_year_on_year⌗ sg.prices.sg_producer_prices
Data Gaps (5)
  • All monthly SingStat/CEIC price and IIP series (retail fuel, CPI, supply/import/export/producer price indices, refining and petrochemicals IIP) are stale by 65 days — April and May 2026 data are not yet available, so the current state of refinery throughput and consumer price pass-through cannot be confirmed.
  • Wholesale trade indices (FWTI petroleum, chemicals, bunkering) are stale by 155 days — no war-period observations are available for these series.
  • All shipping nowcast data are stale by 29 days (latest week: 6 April 2026); more recent Malacca and port-call data are not in the snapshot.
  • Industrial chemical export partner-share data are annual and last updated December 2025 — no war-period shift in export destination mix is observable.
  • No data on Singapore's strategic petroleum reserve levels or refinery utilisation rates are available in this dataset.

Financial markets

Overview

Singapore's financial markets are broadly orderly: USD/SGD has strengthened modestly (SGD appreciated ~1.1% against the pre-conflict average), the NEER is at its war-period high, and SGS yields are within a narrow range of pre-conflict levels. The STI is 9% above the pre-conflict average despite a mild recent pullback, and FX implied volatility has retreated sharply from its early-March peak to near the war-period low. The main signal of note is a surge in SGX equity turnover and FX market activity, which reflects elevated trading volumes rather than stress.

Key Drivers (5)

  • USD/SGD spot is at 1.2817 as of 30 April 2026, approximately 1.1% stronger than the November–December 2025 average of 1.2963, and sitting mid-range within the war period (56.9% through the war range of 1.2694–1.2910). The NEER is at its war-period high (141.62), up 0.85% from the pre-conflict average, consistent with a modestly firmer SGD in trade-weighted terms.

    ⌗ sg.financial_markets.usd_sgd_spot_vs_1m_3m_forwards⌗ sg.financial_markets.effective_exchange_rates_neer_vs_reer

  • USD/SGD 1-month implied volatility (a measure of market-implied uncertainty about the exchange rate) has fallen to 3.93% annualised — near the war-period low (3.87%) and well below the war-period peak of 5.36% reached on 9 March 2026. The 3-month tenor at 4.14% is similarly subdued. Both tenors remain modestly above the pre-conflict average (~3.4% and ~3.7% respectively) but the trend is clearly downward.

    ⌗ sg.financial_markets.usd_sgd_implied_vol_1m_vs_3m

  • SGS 2-year yields are at 1.56% (+15.6 basis points above the pre-conflict average) and 10-year yields at 2.12% (essentially flat vs the pre-conflict average of 2.12%). The yield curve has steepened modestly. Both tenors are well off their war-period peaks (1.69% and 2.41% respectively, reached 27 March 2026) and sit in the middle of their war-period ranges.

    ⌗ sg.financial_markets.sgs_yield_curve_2y_vs_10y⌗ sg.financial_markets.bval_yield_curve_2y_vs_10y

  • The STI stands at 3,821 as of 30 April 2026, 9% above the pre-conflict average of 3,505, though it has pulled back from its war-period high of 3,951 (15 April 2026). SGX daily share turnover is at its war-period high of 3,063 million shares (154% above the pre-conflict average), and FX monthly turnover in March 2026 was 30% above the pre-conflict average — both reflecting elevated activity rather than distress.

    ⌗ sg.financial_markets.straits_times_index_sti⌗ sg.financial_markets.sgx_daily_turnover⌗ sg.financial_markets.forex_monthly_turnover

  • The domestic interbank overnight rate is at 1.31% as of 30 April 2026, at its war-period high and 16.6 basis points above the pre-conflict average, while 3-month compounded SORA at 1.04% is 19.7 basis points below the pre-conflict average. The divergence between the overnight rate and the 3-month compounded rate does not indicate funding stress — the overnight rate's elevation reflects end-of-month liquidity dynamics rather than a sustained tightening of interbank conditions.

    ⌗ sg.financial_markets.domestic_interbank_overnight⌗ sg.financial_markets.sora_3m_compounded
Data Gaps (3)
  • FX monthly turnover data are stale by 65 days (latest: March 2026); April and May 2026 FX activity levels are not observable.
  • No credit spread data (e.g. SGD corporate bond spreads or CDS) are available in this dataset to assess whether credit conditions have tightened alongside the equity and rates moves.
  • No MAS policy band parameters (slope, width, centre) are available in this dataset; the NEER position relative to the current band cannot be assessed.
Zoom

Prices

Singapore retail fuel prices, headline and core inflation, plus the domestic supply / import / export / producer price indices.

Retail fuel prices (monthly)

Monthly retail prices for the three petrol grades plus diesel — official SingStat survey series.

⌗ sg.prices.retail_fuel_prices_monthly

Pump prices (daily)

Daily pump-station prices scraped across grades and brands.

⌗ sg.prices.pump_prices_daily

Electricity tariff (households)

Low-tension domestic tariff.

⌗ sg.prices.electricity_tariff_households

Headline and core CPI (year-on-year)

Year-on-year change in headline CPI and MAS Core inflation (which strips out accommodation and private-transport costs).

⌗ sg.prices.headline_and_core_cpi_year_on_year

Headline and core CPI (month-on-month)

Same headline and core inflation series, month-on-month.

⌗ sg.prices.headline_and_core_cpi_month_on_month

Domestic supply prices

Prices of goods supplied to the Singapore market — split into oil and non-oil components.

⌗ sg.prices.sg_supply_prices

Import prices

Prices of imports landing in Singapore — split into oil, non-oil, and food components.

⌗ sg.prices.sg_import_prices

Export prices

Prices Singapore exporters charge overseas buyers — split into oil and non-oil.

⌗ sg.prices.sg_export_prices

Producer prices

Factory-gate prices for goods manufactured in Singapore (excludes imports), split into oil and non-oil.

⌗ sg.prices.sg_producer_prices

Construction material prices

Monthly prices of key construction materials — cement, steel bars, granite, and concreting sand.

⌗ sg.prices.construction_prices

Sectoral economic activity

Real-side activity indicators across the sectors most exposed to upstream energy cost shocks: refining/petrochemicals, wholesale, transport, construction, real estate, and F&B.

Petroleum refining

Index of industrial production for refinery throughput.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.petroleum_refining

Petrochemicals

Industrial production for the Jurong Island petrochemicals complex.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.petrochemicals

Specialty and other chemicals

Industrial production for specialty (paints, coatings, adhesives) and other basic chemicals.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.specialty_and_other_chemicals

Wholesale trade

Foreign Wholesale Trade Index — overall plus petroleum, chemicals, and ship-chandlers/bunkering subsectors.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.wholesale_trade

Sea cargo handled

Total sea cargo handled at the Port of Singapore (thousand tons).

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.sea_cargo_handled

Container throughput

Container throughput at Singapore port (thousand TEU).

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.container_throughput

Changi Airport flight movements

Monthly count of aircraft movements at Changi.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.changi_airport_flight_movements

Changi Airport passenger movements

Monthly passenger throughput at Changi.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.changi_airport_passenger_movements

Changi Airport air freight

Monthly air-freight tonnage handled at Changi.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.changi_airport_air_freight

Cross-border land arrivals

Visitor arrivals into Singapore by land.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.cross_border_land_arrivals

Construction contracts awarded

Monthly value of contracts awarded, public and private.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.construction_contracts_awarded

Construction material demand

Monthly physical-volume demand for cement, steel bars, and granite.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.construction_material_demand

Private property price index

URA quarterly benchmark for non-HDB housing prices.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.private_property_price_index

Property deals (developer sales + resales)

URA monthly count of private residential transactions.

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.property_deals_developer_sales_resales

Food and beverage services

F&B Services Index — overall plus five segments (restaurants, fast food, caterers, food courts, cafes).

⌗ sg.sectoral_activity.food_and_beverage_services

Mineral fuel imports — Singapore import market share by partner

Each card shows Singapore's import market share by partner country (left axis, 100% stacked bars), with a red line on the right axis tracking the total share of the six affected Middle East countries: UAE, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, Bahrain. Periods include annual averages (2023–2025) and recent monthly snapshots, with a pre-war Jan–Feb 2026 average for direct comparison against post-war months.

Mineral fuels

SITC 3 — all mineral fuels and related materials (crude, refined products, gas, coal, electric current).

⌗ sg.trade.trade_pshare_sitc3
Crude petroleum oils

SITC 333 — unrefined crude oil, feedstock for Singapore's Jurong Island refineries (processed into gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, naphtha).

⌗ sg.trade.trade_pshare_sitc333
Refined petroleum products

SITC 334 — already-refined fuels (gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, naphtha, fuel oil). Singapore also imports refined products to blend, re-export, and supply bunker fuel — the trading-hub side of refining activity.

⌗ sg.trade.trade_pshare_sitc334
Natural gas (all forms)

SITC 343 — natural gas in both pipeline and liquefied form. Singapore is almost entirely import-dependent for gas, which fuels nearly all power generation. Qatar dominates regional LNG supply.

⌗ sg.trade.trade_pshare_sitc343
Naphtha

SITC 3346043 — naphtha, cracked into ethylene/propylene for plastics and used as a gasoline blendstock. Singapore is a major regional trading hub with key Middle Eastern suppliers.

⌗ sg.trade.trade_pshare_sitc3346043
Liquefied natural gas (LNG)

SITC 3431 — LNG specifically (vs SITC 3432 pipeline gas). Imported into the SLNG terminal on Jurong Island; Qatar is the dominant Middle Eastern supplier.

⌗ sg.trade.trade_pshare_sitc3431000

Singapore exports — regional dependence

Where Singapore's exports of these refining-and-petrochemical products go. Each card pairs annual % shares (2023–2025, left) with monthly export levels in SGD thousands (right), broken out by the same 10 regional destinations as the import-share charts, plus an 'Others' residual covering non-regional destinations (mainly US/EU).

Industrial chemicals

SITC 5 (chemicals and related products) less SITC 51 (organic chemicals) less SITC 54 (medicinal and pharmaceutical products) — the basic industrial-chemicals subset most exposed to upstream cost pressure from Middle East energy supply disruption.

ChinaIndiaIndonesiaJapanMalaysiaPhilippinesSouth KoreaTaiwanThailandVietnamOthers

Monthly levels

⌗ sg.trade.industrial_chemicals

Oil

SITC 3 — all mineral fuels and related materials (crude, refined products, gas, coal, electric current). Captures the full re-export-and-refining value chain through the Singapore hub.

Monthly levels

⌗ sg.trade.oil

Refined petroleum products

SITC 334 — already-refined fuels (gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, naphtha, fuel oil) re-exported from Jurong Island after blending and processing. The narrow refining-margin slice of SITC 3.

Annual shares (2023–2025)

Monthly levels

⌗ sg.trade.refined_petroleum_products

Malacca Strait — total weekly transits

Singapore sits at the eastern end of the Malacca Strait — most of SG's seaborne trade passes through it. Tracking Malacca transits gives an early warning signal for upstream disruption before it shows up in SG port calls.

Malacca Strait weekly transits

Weekly vessel transits through the Malacca Strait, all vessel types combined. Solid blue is actual; dashed amber is the counterfactual estimate of what transits would have been absent the war.

⌗ sg.shipping.malacca_strait_weekly_transits

Singapore port calls — overview

Singapore's total weekly port calls — actual versus the counterfactual estimate of what calls would have been absent the war.

Total port calls

Weekly count of all vessel types arriving at Singapore (imports plus exports). Solid blue is actual; dashed amber is the counterfactual primary estimate.

⌗ sg.shipping.total_port_calls

Singapore shipping activity — by vessel type

Vessel-type drill-down for the two categories most directly exposed to Iran-related disruption: tankers (energy trade) and containers (general goods). Each card shows three weekly metrics — port calls (vessel count), import tonnage (cargo unloaded), and export tonnage (cargo loaded) — so the war effect can be traced to vessel traffic, inbound cargo, or outbound cargo separately.

Tankers

Vessels carrying crude, refined products, LNG, and chemicals. Sub-charts split out vessel calls and inbound/outbound cargo tonnage.

Port calls (count)

Import tonnage (cargo unloaded)

Export tonnage (cargo loaded)

⌗ sg.shipping.tankers

Containers

General-merchandise vessel traffic. Sub-charts split out vessel calls and inbound/outbound cargo tonnage.

Port calls (count)

Import tonnage (cargo unloaded)

Export tonnage (cargo loaded)

⌗ sg.shipping.containers

Foreign exchange

USD/SGD spot, forward curve, effective exchange rates, and option-implied volatility. The MAS NEER is the policy band's reference; REER strips out domestic vs. trade-partner inflation to give a real competitiveness view.

USD/SGD — spot vs 1M / 3M forwards

Daily SGD/USD spot, 1-month and 3-month forwards. Forward premium widens when SGD rates drop relative to USD (FX-implied rate differential).

⌗ sg.financial_markets.usd_sgd_spot_vs_1m_3m_forwards

Effective exchange rates — NEER vs REER

Daily Singapore Nominal Effective Exchange Rate (NEER) and Real Effective Exchange Rate (REER), trade-weighted indices vs MAS basket. NEER is the policy band reference; REER deflates by relative inflation.

⌗ sg.financial_markets.effective_exchange_rates_neer_vs_reer

USD/SGD implied vol — 1M vs 3M

Daily option-implied volatility on USD/SGD pair, 1-month and 3-month tenors.

⌗ sg.financial_markets.usd_sgd_implied_vol_1m_vs_3m

Forex monthly turnover

Monthly Singapore FX market turnover (SGD millions) — MAS Survey of Forex Market Activity. Includes spot, forwards, swaps, options, and other derivatives across all currency pairs traded out of Singapore.

⌗ sg.financial_markets.forex_monthly_turnover

Interest rates

Singapore short-end and long-end interest rates. Iran/Hormuz energy shocks tend to push longer yields higher (term-premium repricing on inflation risk) and money-market rates higher (USD funding stress passing through to SGD via FX-implied rates).

SGS yield curve — 2Y vs 10Y

Daily yields on Singapore Government Securities, % per annum. The 2Y–10Y spread captures the slope of the curve.

⌗ sg.financial_markets.sgs_yield_curve_2y_vs_10y

BVAL yield curve — 2Y vs 10Y

Bloomberg Valuation (BVAL) SGS yields, daily. Independent market-implied curve — small basis vs the MAS reference yields above.

⌗ sg.financial_markets.bval_yield_curve_2y_vs_10y

SORA 3M compounded

Singapore Overnight Rate Average compounded over 3 months — the post-2024 reference rate for SGD-denominated lending.

⌗ sg.financial_markets.sora_3m_compounded

Domestic interbank — overnight

Singapore domestic interbank average overnight rate, daily. The shortest end of the SGD money-market curve.

⌗ sg.financial_markets.domestic_interbank_overnight

Equity markets

Singapore equity market gauges. STI is the headline 30-stock blue-chip index; SGX turnover captures volume. Risk-off episodes typically show STI dropping with elevated turnover.

Straits Times Index (STI)

Daily Straits Times Index — 30 largest SGX-listed companies by market cap. Headline benchmark for SG equity performance.

⌗ sg.financial_markets.straits_times_index_sti

SGX daily turnover

Daily SGX equity turnover, millions of shares.

⌗ sg.financial_markets.sgx_daily_turnover

MAS network access required

This report is hosted on the MAS team SharePoint site (team.dms.mas.gov.sg) and requires you to be connected to the MAS network or VPN to open.
